titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Jeffrey Sammons Papers MC.370
titlestmt.author: Accessioned by Lauren Stark and Processed by Stacey Flatt, 2026

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-03-30 13:15:02 UTC.

physdesc.extent: in 4 manuscript boxes and 2 cassette boxes

did.abstract: Jeffrey T. Sammons (1949- ) is a historian, professor, and writer specializing in Black history and the history of sport. He taught at New York University (NYU) from 1989 until his retirement as professor emeritus in 2022, where he also served as director of graduate studies. This collection (1986–2017) documents his research and professional activities, particularly his teaching, speaking engagements, and event organizing. The collection includes materials from the 1998 Sport Matters Symposium, organized in response to Darwin's Athletes by John Hoberman; documentation from the 2000 American Historians Annual Conference, including paper files and a video recording of the "Make Racism History" demonstration and the conference; work files and audio recordings from NYU's Faculty Resource Network Summer Program (1996–2004); and a small number of teaching-related materials including syllabi, committee records, and Institute of African American Affairs files. Also included are material accumulated on Sammons' involvement in NYU's 2001 acknowledgment of The Bates Seven (students expelled in 1941 for protesting the benching of a Black football player during a 1940 game at the University of Missouri).
did.physloc: Materials are stored offsite and advance notice is required for use. Please request materials at least two business days prior to your research visit to coordinate access.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Administrative records and unpublished reports of New York University are closed for a period of 20 years from the date of their creation. Access to files spanning multiple years will be opened to researchers based on the date of the most recent materials. Board of Trustees records are closed for 35 years from the date of creation. Materials related to personnel, grievances, job and fellowship searches and applications, and all files that fall under the federal Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) are permanently restricted. Additional restrictions may apply to other materials in this collection. For questions regarding specific restrictions, please contact the University Archives.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Donated by Jeffrey Sammons, October 15, 2022. The accession number associated with this donation is 2022.87.

appraisal.head: Appraisal
appraisal.p:
One poster was appraised out of the collection during accessioning due to the presence of an identical, smaller copy in collection.
In March 2026, the following were removed from the collection: NYU publications, duplicates, New York Times articles, and 1 cd.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Files in this collection are arranged in chronological order, with similar materials grouped together.

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Jeffrey T. Sammons (1949- ) is a professor, historian, and writer. He earned his B.A. in history at Rutgers College, a masters degree in history from Tufts University, and a PhD in American History from the University of North Carolina. He taught history at New York University (NYU) starting in 1989, specializing in Black history and sport until he was appointed professor emeritus upon his retirement in 2022. During Sammons' time at NYU, he also served as the director of graduate studies. He has also taught at Princeton University and at Hollins College (Roanoke, Va.). Sammons has written widely on the subject of sport and race, and has participated in and consulted on numerous documentary projects in film and on television.

phystech.head: Audiovisual Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Audiovisual materials have not been preserved and may not be available to researchers. Materials not yet digitized will need to have access copies made before they can be used. To request an access copy, or if you are unsure if an item has been digitized, please contact New York University Archives, special.collections@nyu.edu, 212-998-2596, with the collection name, collection number, and a description of the item(s) requested. A staff member will respond to you with further information.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date; Jeffrey Sammons Papers; MC 370; box number; folder number or item identifier; New York University Archives, New York University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p:
Materials were placed in new acid-free boxes. Born digital items were identified, separated, and inventoried. New York University Libraries follow professional standards and best practices when imaging, ingesting, and processing born-digital material in order to maintain the integrity and authenticity of the content.
In March 2026, materials were placed into acid-free legal folders. Duplicate publications, documents, and publications widely available online or in NYU's catalog holdings were removed. Audiovisual material was numbered, labeled, and listed individually within the inventory. A CD containing 112 digital photographs of the 2010 University of North Carolina Phi Beta Kappa induction ceremony, with speaker Julius Chambers was reviewed during processing, was determined to fall outside the scope of the collection and was removed.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
The Jeffrey Sammons Papers (1986–2017) document Sammons' research on Black history and the history of sport, as reflected in his teaching and professional activities both at New York University (NYU) and beyond. The collection includes planning files for the Sport Matters Symposium at NYU in 1998, organized by Sammons in response to opinions presented in the book Darwin's Athletes by John Hoberman. These files contain event schedules, correspondence, funding materials, notes, and symposium papers.
Planning files from the 2000 American Historians Annual Conference in St. Louis are also in this collection. These files consist of correspondence, notes, and a conference program. This material documents the responses by conference members when racial discrimination allegations were made towards the conference hotel. This information led to Sammons and others organizing a "Make Racism History" demonstration during the conference. The collection also contains a video recording of the demonstration and portions of the conference.
Sammons' involvement in NYU's Faculty Resource Network Summer Program (1996–2004) is documented through correspondence, programs, schedules, and audio recordings of discussions and presentations. Additionally, a small number of files relate to his teaching in NYU's History Department including course syllabi, Africana Studies records, Staffing Committee meeting notes and communications, and materials documenting his role in NYU's 2001 acknowledgment of The Bates Seven (students expelled in 1941 for protesting the benching of a Black football player during a 1940 game at the University of Missouri.)

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: This collection is protected by copyright and/or related rights. You are free to use materials in the collection in any way that is permitted by the copyright and related rights legislation that applies to your use. For other uses you need to obtain permission from the rights-holder(s).

controlaccess.corpname: Faculty Resource Network at New York University

did.unittitle: Darlene Clark Hine NYU Lecture Event Planning -- Introduction Text
Africana Studies Program -- Class Information, Funding, Proposed Africa House
Faculty Resource Network Summer Program Audio Recordings

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Because most tapes are not labeled with a year, they have been arranged by month and day. The recordings likely span multiple years, approximately 1996–1998, although only a small number of tapes include specific year information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These audiocassettes were recorded during NYU's Faculty Resource Network Summer Programs and document sessions from "Rethinking African-American History and Literature Through Autobiography," held across multiple years, approximately 1996 to 1998. Label titles reflect the full or partial names of speakers, lecture topics, or a combination of both. Notations of "AM" and "PM" indicate the time of day the session was recorded. A semicolon (;) within a title separates the label text corresponding to each side of the audiocassette, when necessary.
